headline: New 'Faces of Death' Film Examines Digital Voyeurism and Reality Horror Legacy
deck: A contemporary cinematic interpretation of the 1978 cult film explores the enduring societal appetite for graphic content in the internet age.
body: A new feature film, starring Barbie Ferreira and Dacre Montgomery, revisits the thematic underpinnings of the 1978 mondo-horror cult film “Faces of Death.” This production functions as a slasher-movie meditation on its predecessor, engaging with the persistent cultural fascination with simulated graphic content and its modern digital amplification.

The original 1978 “Faces of Death” emerged as a prominent example of exploitation cinema, presenting itself as a documentary featuring authentic scenes of human and animal fatalities. Despite its claims, the film was largely a fabrication, a fact that did not impede its commercial success. It grossed $35 million internationally and solidified its status as a significant cult artifact during the VHS era.

This phenomenon was not isolated; it was rooted in a broader cultural shift of the 1970s. The Manson murders are frequently cited as a catalyst for a “reality-horror revolution,” influencing slasher films such as “The Texas Chain Saw Massacre” to evoke a sense of a 'psychotic nightmare made flesh.' This era followed earlier genre benchmarks, with Alfred Hitchcock’s “Psycho,” released in 1960, having previously established a foundational slasher narrative.

The deceptive nature of the 1978 film was a key component of its allure. “The truth? 'Faces of Death' was almost entirely a fake. The 'real' murders it depicted were staged movie murders presented in grimy nonfiction drag,” according to one analysis.

The new “Faces of Death,” directed and co-written by Daniel Goldhaber, diverges from its antecedent by not being a remake or another mockumentary. Instead, it is structured as a retro slasher film, deliberately emulating the aesthetic of a '70s grindhouse movie. Its narrative centers on a killer who meticulously restages murders and executions from the original film, subsequently disseminating these acts online, effectively transforming homicide into "ultimate clickbait.

This contemporary adaptation directly addresses the evolution of media consumption and the commodification of extreme violence. Director Goldhaber is noted for updating the concept of a "cursed video tape" for a digital epoch. “Daniel Goldhaber updates a cursed video tape for an age when we all walk around with snuff films in our pockets,” IndieWire observed, highlighting the ubiquitous access to graphic content in the modern era.

The film provocatively explores the notion that the antagonist, a killer named Arthur, operates not merely as a serial murderer but as an integral component of the “new anything-goes attention economy.” This perspective suggests a symbiotic relationship between extreme acts and the insatiable demand for online engagement.

One critical assessment noted, “The idea that a sicko like Arthur isn’t just a serial killer — he’s part of the new anything-goes attention economy! — is a provocative but facile idea.” This commentary underscores the film's ambition to connect individual pathology with broader societal trends in digital consumption.

Through its narrative, the new “Faces of Death” scrutinizes the enduring human propensity for voyeurism and the blurring distinctions between staged and authentic violence. It serves as a commentary on how the hunger for forbidden content, once satisfied by niche cult films, is now continually fed through pervasive online platforms, reflecting a persistent cultural engagement with the morbid and the transgressive.
